alsoKnownAs: Symphysodon discus (traditional Heckel — see taxonomic note)

nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to still blackwater rivers, flooded igapó forest margins, lake inlets, and sheltered backwaters of the Rio Negro system and eastern Amazon tributaries; almost exclusively associated with submerged roots, branches, and leaf litter in extremely soft, humic-acid-stained blackwater with minimal flow and very low conductivity (9–13 µS/cm)

decorPreferences: large driftwood roots and branches for shelter and territory definition
Indian almond (Catappa) leaves and alder cones to release tannins and lower pH
peat filtration or blackwater extract to replicate Rio Negro humic chemistry
fine dark sand substrate (allows natural foraging behaviour — fish 'blow' into substrate to find invertebrates)
dim to subdued lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) or tall background plants to diffuse light
minimal to no flow — gentle turnover only; strong currents stress these fish
open mid-water swimming space central to the tank
clay spawning cones or broad Amazon sword leaves for potential spawning

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous/carnivorous in the wild: insect larvae (especially chironomid/mosquito larvae), small crustaceans, planktonic invertebrates, and worms; organic and vegetal matter also consumed, especially in the dry season. In the aquarium, offer a variety of high-protein foods: frozen bloodworms (black and white mosquito larvae), frozen brine shrimp, frozen daphnia, frozen mysis, and live or frozen Artemia nauplii. High-quality discus-specific pellets or granules accepted by established specimens. Beef-heart mixes sometimes used but should not be the sole diet. Feed small portions 2–3 times daily; remove uneaten food promptly — water quality is critical and deteriorates rapidly. Wild-caught individuals may initially refuse prepared foods and require live foods to initiate feeding.

goodWith: cardinal tetras (Paracheirodon axelrodi) — classic blackwater biotope companion at matching temperature
other discus (conspecifics — kept in groups of 6+)
Corydoras sterbai (highest temperature tolerance among corydoras, compatible with warm blackwater)
peaceful South American tetras tolerant of warm, very soft, acid water
avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous species that will stress discus
common plecos (disruptive, compete for food at night)
invertebrates (shrimp risk being eaten; copper medications fatal to inverts and harmful to discus)
fast, hyperactive species that outcompete discus at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo for Symphysodon discus. Verify current URL availability and confirm the Heckel bar (prominent 5th vertical bar) is visible before use.
Lateral view of Symphysodon discus showing the diagnostic prominent 5th vertical Heckel bar. Verify availability and photographer attribution before use.

summary: The Heckel Discus is the hardest discus to maintain — a Rio Negro blackwater specialist demanding pH 4.5–6.0, extremely soft water, and near-perfect husbandry. Distinguished by a dramatically prominent 5th vertical bar (the 'Heckel bar'). Almost exclusively wild-collected; a true expert-level species.
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE: The assignment of the trade name 'Heckel Discus' to a scientific species is genuinely contested. The traditional and most widely used name is Symphysodon discus Heckel, 1840 — the type species of the genus, described from Rio Negro specimens. The Schultz (2004/2006) revision, widely cited in hobby literature, recognised three species: S. discus (Heckel), S. aequifasciatus (Common/Brown/Blue Discus — see fw-fish-166), and S. tarzoo (Red/Tefé Discus). However, Géry and Bleher (2004) elevated the former subspecies S. aequifasciata haraldi to full species rank as S. haraldi, applying it to the brown/blue discus complex rather than to the Heckel form; Bleher et al. (2007) subsequently supported a three-species model — S. discus, S. aequifasciatus, and S. haraldi — with S. tarzoo synonymised under S. aequifasciatus. A 2011 molecular study (Amado, Farias, Hrbek) identified five evolutionary lineages, not clearly resolving species boundaries, and does not recognise S. haraldi as valid. The Catalog of Fishes and some databases (including FishBase records) treat S. haraldi as a fourth valid species distinct from both S. discus and S. aequifasciatus. This entry uses S. haraldi as the scientific name per the assignment ID provided (fw-fish-373), but hobby and scientific literature predominantly refer to the 'Heckel Discus' as S. discus; cross-referencing both names is essential when consulting sources. The diagnostic 'Heckel bar' — the dramatically widened and darkened 5th of 9 vertical bars running through the centre of the body — is present only on S. discus and is never seen on S. aequifasciatus or S. haraldi sensu Bleher. This file documents the trade fish sold as 'Heckel Discus' characterised by that prominent central bar.

The Heckel Discus is widely regarded as the most challenging discus to keep in captivity, more demanding than the Common Discus (fw-fish-166) even for experienced discus keepers. The species is endemic to blackwater systems — principally the Rio Negro and tributaries (Rio Nhamundá, Rio Trombetas, upper Uatumã, Abacaxis River) — where pH regularly measures 3.5–5.0, conductivity runs 9–13 µS/cm (barely above rainwater), and the water is deeply stained with humic and fulvic acids from decaying organic matter. Replicating this chemistry is not optional; Heckel discus maintained in higher-pH or harder water suffer chronic stress, suppressed immune function, and shortened lifespan.

Water preparation: Reverse osmosis water remineralised to near-zero hardness is strongly recommended. Target pH 4.8–5.5 for long-term keeping, with tannin supplementation via Indian almond leaves, Catappa bark, alder cones, and/or peat filtration. Do not use carbonate buffering; kH should be near zero. Conductivity target 30–80 µS/cm.

Temperature must be maintained at 28–32°C (82–90°F) consistently; wild populations experience daily peaks to 34°C and are never exposed to temperatures below 26°C. Stability is critical — even brief cold shocks trigger immune suppression and disease outbreaks.

A group of 6 or more is the recommended minimum; hierarchy and pair formation depend on group size. Tank footprint of 150 cm × 50 cm minimum is required. Fine dark sand substrate is preferred — discus forage by 'blowing' jets of water into substrate to flush out invertebrates. Keep flow minimal; strong current is stressful.

The Heckel Discus is almost exclusively wild-caught. Captive breeding is possible but very rarely accomplished successfully by hobbyists, as it requires extreme blackwater conditioning (pH 5.5–6.5 for tank-bred stock, but typically lower for wild fish), appropriate spawning surfaces, and immaculate water quality. Wild-caught specimens require live food (bloodworms, artemia) to initiate feeding in a new tank and may refuse prepared foods for extended periods. Feeding difficulty is rated high.

Disease sensitivity is elevated relative to other discus: hole-in-the-head (HITH/HLLE) is triggered by temperatures below 27°C or nitrate accumulation. Hexamita and other internal parasites are common in wild-caught individuals and must be treated promptly. Copper-based medications are toxic and must never be used. Nitrate should be kept below 10 ppm; nitrite and ammonia at zero at all times. Frequent large water changes (30–50%, 2–3 times per week) with temperature- and pH-matched water are essential.
breedingNotes:
Rarely accomplished successfully in the aquarium; the Heckel Discus is considered the most difficult discus species to breed in captivity. Wild-caught specimens, which represent most of the available stock, carry internal parasites and require quarantine and conditioning before breeding attempts are made. Pairs form naturally from established groups rather than by forced pairing. A dedicated bare-bottom breeding tank of 300+ litres with daily 30–50% water changes using precisely conditioned blackwater is required.

Water parameters for breeding attempts should target pH 5.5–6.5 (somewhat raised from long-term keeping levels to improve egg viability), gH 0–2, temperature 29–31°C, and near-zero nitrate. Spawning surfaces include vertical clay cones, broad Amazon sword leaves, or smooth vertical slate. Clutch size is typically 75–300 eggs; incubation at 29–30°C runs approximately 58–60 hours. Both parents fan and guard the clutch. Fry are wrigglers for approximately 3 days and then become free-swimming; newly free-swimming fry immediately begin grazing on the parental skin-mucus secretions ('discus milk') that both parents produce — this obligate parental contact must not be interrupted for at least 10–14 days. First-time pairs almost invariably consume eggs; repeat spawning attempts with the same pair typically improve. Breedability in captivity is rated advanced to 'not in captivity' for wild-caught stock under normal hobby conditions.

primary.name: FishBase — Symphysodon discus
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological source. Provided: species as S. discus Heckel, 1840 (type species); maximum size 20 cm TL (note: FishBase also lists 12.3 cm SL as a measured specimen, but 20 cm TL is the accepted maximum); temperature 26–30°C; pH 4.2–6.2; hardness 0–1 dH (extremely soft); native range: Amazon River basin in Brazil, lower Rio Negro, lower Abacaxis River, lower Trombetas River; diet: worms, crustaceans, insects, plant matter; trophic level 2.9; IUCN Least Concern (2022); breeding: hundreds of eggs on stones or plants, biparental care, fry feed on skin mucus. FishBase also cross-lists S. haraldi (Schultz, 1960) as valid, associated with eastern Amazon localities distinct from the Rio Negro S. discus.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Symphysodon discus
additional.notes: Confirmed distribution: lower Rio Negro, upper Uatumã, Nhamundá, Trombetas, Abacaxis rivers; restricted to blackwater; temperature 26–32°C; pH 4.2–5.2; maximum 20 cm; IUCN Least Concern 2022. Notes S. discus willischwartzi (Burgess, 1981) as a synonym. Does not address S. haraldi relationship.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser GmbH — Symphysodon discus
additional.notes: German specialist importer source. Confirmed: almost exclusively wild-collected; rarely bred in captivity; must be kept in groups of at least 6 (8–12 preferred); humic substances from peat, alder cones, and dead leaves critical for immune defence; fine sand substrate for natural foraging; subdued lighting essential. Wild localities cited include Rio Negro, Suncunduri, Rio Nhamundá, Mari Cross, Rio Madeira, Nova Olinda. Provides taxonomic context: follows Bleher et al. (2007) three-species model (S. discus, S. aequifasciatus, S. haraldi) treating S. tarzoo as synonym of S. aequifasciatus.

additional.name: SimplyDiscus Forum — Wild Rio Negro Conditions Information (Heckel Project)
additional.notes: Field measurement data from Rio Negro Heckel collection sites. pH 3.5–5.0, most commonly 4.0–4.5; conductivity 9–13 µS/cm; temperature 80–86°F (26.7–30°C) typical with daily peaks to 34.3°C; dissolved oxygen 5.0–6.5 mg/L; water brown with high turbidity from dissolved organic content; collection depths typically under 5 feet. Used to calibrate pH and gH lower bounds and temperature upper bound.

additional.name: Garnelio.de — Discus Heckel DNZ (Symphysodon discus) care sheet
additional.notes: European specialist retailer. Provided: temperature 28–31°C; pH 3.5–7.5 (published range very wide; lower end reflects wild blackwater); gH 0–15, kH 0–4; minimum tank 150 cm length, ~400–450 litres for 5 fish; diet: discus pellets, live mosquito larvae, artemia, daphnia, frozen foods, vegetable matter; feed multiple small portions daily; care level 3 (difficult/advanced); breeding difficult, requires spawning cone, parental fanning. Compatible with red neons, corydoras, butterfly cichlids, tetras.

additional.name: Amado, Farias & Hrbek (2011) — A Molecular Perspective on Systematics, Taxonomy and Classification of Amazonian Discus Fishes (PMC3147135)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed molecular study identifying five evolutionary significant units in Symphysodon. Recognises S. discus (Heckel phenotype) as distinct; does not validate S. haraldi as a separate species from the brown/blue complex; the blue phenotype may represent an undescribed species. Microsatellite data show weak divergence between blue and brown phenotypes despite distinct mtDNA haplotype groups and differing habitat pH/conductivity. Key reference for the ongoing taxonomic uncertainty documented in the care notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner; fry depend on obligate parental skin-mucus ('discus milk') for the first 10–14 days post-hatching. Clutch 75–300 eggs; incubation ~58–60 hours at 29–30°C. Extreme blackwater conditions required (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 0–2, near-zero kH). Wild-caught stock must be dewormed/conditioned before breeding attempts; first spawns almost always fail. Captive breeding is very rarely successful — most Heckel discus in trade are wild-collected.
